nodes.label: AI as initial research filter for broad direction
nodes.label_negative: AI as source of final accurate answers
nodes.grounding_span: AI was useful for general guidance but specifics were ordinarily proven incorrect

nodes.label: AI reliability for well-established older research
nodes.label_negative: AI reliability for recent specific research
nodes.grounding_span: AI's strength was in quoting past research that was over 10 years ago and is still being cited today. It is not particularly accurate in quoting recent research

nodes.label: AI as presentation and communication format advisor
nodes.label_negative: AI as content generator for scientific specifics
nodes.grounding_span: Several students used AI to find the best format to present their findings and actually found some good guidance here

nodes.label: Student verification diligence in AI use
nodes.label_negative: Student uncritical acceptance of AI output
nodes.grounding_span: The students that approached this the way I would have done, used the AI for initial general direction and then used this to craft search terms for the major databases

nodes.label: AI trustworthiness in high-stakes medical contexts
nodes.grounding_span: It takes a lot of confidence, particularly in an area such as medicine, to say to students 'you can trust what this AI is saying'

nodes.label: Efficient research process
nodes.grounding_span: The students found AI useful to 'whittle down' this initial conventional step

nodes.label: Student learning and critical thinking

nodes.label: Negative view of AI for specific recent research
Cautious stance on AI trust in medicine

nodes.label: Verification-first approach — always cross-check AI claims with trusted databases
Task-appropriate delegation — assign AI only to tasks where it is reliable
